title: How to Make a Marvel Movie Funny With 'Thor: Ragnarok' Director Taika Waititi | The Big Picture (Ep. 32)
format: Sean Fennessey talks to Taika Waititi about turning the third Thor movie into a comedy, being left alone by Marvel, and how Korg's rock-paper-scissors joke got past the filter.

films.title: Thor: Ragnarok
films.note: Marvel came to him mid-edit on 'Hunt for the Wilderpeople'; he pitched Cate Blanchett a chamber piece about a man trying to get home while an arsonist burns down his house. He treated the script as a blueprint and riffed scenes over 20 takes, and he'd put the Led Zeppelin cut in his pitch reel before anyone cleared the song.

films.title: Boy
films.note: The film that actually got Marvel interested, he says, because it proved he could work pathos into the comedy rather than just tell jokes.

films.title: Hunt for the Wilderpeople
films.note: He was halfway through the edit, in Hawaii after the birth of his second child, when the Thor email arrived.

films.title: What We Do in the Shadows
films.note: The vampire mockumentary with Jemaine Clement; its cop spin-off is what pulled him back to New Zealand right after Ragnarok.

films.title: Guardians of the Galaxy
films.note: One of his two favorite Marvel franchises. He says he'd never make a Guardians film because James Gunn already owns it so completely.

films.title: Iron Man
films.note: His other favorite. He suspects it was shot the way he likes to work, actors listening and riffing rather than reciting canned one-liners.

films.title: Carol
films.note: Cate Blanchett came straight off it and wanted to fight and wear a funny costume for once; Waititi needled her about it on set, pitching 'Carol 2.'

films.title: Get Out
films.note: His pick for the last great thing he saw. He watched it with his mouth hanging open, he says, the same expression Daniel Kaluuya gives throughout.

What We Do in the Shadows.title: What We Do in the Shadows
